Proposed data are initially reviewed internally by the science team. If approved for  
consideration, then the data are nominated for review by the User Working Group  
(UWG). If approved, a dissemination plan is proposed to the UWG. If approved, the  
data are archived and curated, collaboratively with the data producers, to develop a  
product or service. The completed data product or service then receives alpha review.  
Upon completion of required revisions and approval by the Configuration Management  
Board (CMB), the data product or service receives an external beta review, which  
includes the UWG and invited peer-reviewers. Upon completion of required revisions,  
archiving of a new version, if applicable, and approval by the CMB, the data are  
released to the public. Comments received about the data after release are presented to  
the science team.

So, for example, we've developed a tool. So, in the field  
where we work, over 20 years ago we were one of the first to develop a category of data that we  
call gridded population data using GIS tools. But over the years, a lot of other groups have  
developed comparable data, but using new methods and different input data. And we still do ours 
because it's kind of a simple, clean way of doing it, but other people have taken our data and  
enhanced it and, and others or use different methods completely from ours. So, it actually gets  
confusing to users as to which data set is most appropriate and they all have their pros and cons.  
So, we've actually have developed a site that allows people to find comparable information.  
Because the scientist is fine going and reading a paper about a dataset but other kinds of users  
aren't going to read eight different papers from totally different journals and try to make sense of  
it. So we've developed comparable metadata. We have a tool that allows you to view the same  
region and compare what each of the different datasets tell you. And that lets users decide, helps 

users decide, what dataset might be the best one for their purpose

And, when I say good, what I mean by good is that the program or whatever tools you build to

So that's what I think a lot,  
you know, we're moving from a world that used to have - where data was scarce on any

Generally, when people asked me about what licenses they should  
use, if it's data that they don't mind putting out there and it's something that they want to make  
free and open. Then personally, I like the Creative Commons license with attributions. So the  
CC-BY license. And the reason I like that is that it lets anyone who wants to modify the data,  
modify the data. So, there's not a restriction on modification of the data, but also require them to  
document the changes. And I think for what we do, that's something that's really important  
because it gives us protection for the types of data that we put out. We would put out data about  
agricultural markets. It's a trillion plus industry. And people who read the reports, and when we  
look at who reads the reports, you get some pretty big names reading reports, international  
brands, council UN reads the reports. And what we don't want is to have our data go out there,  
someone modifies the data, leaves our logo on it and doesn't document the changes. And then we 
get some change in that data that's attributed to us when it shouldn't have been attributed to us.  
So, you know, it's not a lot of protection, but it's sort of covers us in the case that if someone  
goes and modifies your data, technically our licenses say that they should have documented that  
change.

For that system, every eight days we go out, we  
collect every single image that's been taken of the Earth's surface over that eight-day period,  
comes in 292 tiles covering 180 west, 190 east, 91 to 190 South. And we take those and we  
combine them into one big global. I mentioned that one big global image compressed ends up  
being about four gigabytes per image. But what we do with that image is that we internally tie all 
those so that the arrangements of the pixels within the image itself or actually in blocks, so  
blocks of 256x256 tiles within the image. And what that allows us to do is that when someone  
requests a subset of that image, we can go find those blocks that just overlap that one specific  
area and only pull out those blocks. We don't need to store the original 292 tiles anymore. And  
this is a really efficient structure for us because it means that when we have more. We have more 
pixels in those blocks. We actually get a better compression ratio with the type of compression  
that we're using. And it's that makes the entire structure more space efficient. And then when  
people ask for that data, it actually means that we can create fewer API calls because we're not  
pulling from 292 tiles, we're just pulling from one tile and we're spending, we're sent him at one  
response instead of 292 responses. So when you're working in the Cloud, you get billed per  
request. And that adds up a little bit over time. And it's not a huge cost, but it's something that  
adds up. But it's also something where it reduces the number of socket connections that we need  
to have to that file or to that, to that system at any one time. Which means that we can support  
more traffic from different sources rather than having one person trying to download ten files at a 
time and eating up all of our bandwidth. So then, you know, that doesn't mean we can only have  
one connection to the file at a time. And actually if we, if we're doing stuff internally where we  
need to access the file faster, we can use that same internal tiling structure to essentially retile the 
image and create multiple parallel connections at the same time. So for us, this is all stuff that  
wouldn't be possible if we weren't using a file size that without good compression, probably  
would not be acceptable if you were to try to send it over HDP either. They're rather large files,  
uncompressed. These things are well into the hundreds of gigabytes of storage that they can use.  
So, it's not the kind of stuff that you want to have any excess in terms of in terms of storage  
requirement if you can avoid it. So, for us, large files there are the way to go for us

What my group has done in practice is use two tools.  
And I make that mandatory for all my students. The first is an electronic lab notebook. They  
have to use that. That's basically their data management. And it's a specialized one in  
computational chemistry. So every, every experiment they run, in a computational sense is  
recorded by this electronic lab notebook. And the last action ELN can take is to when you press a 
little button called Publish. And that generates the metadata using a workflow and sends it off to  
a second tool called the institutional repository. And it assigns a DOI. The metadata is registered  
with DataCite, and these DOIs are then cited in all publications, all PhD theses and all other  
documents, in the PowerPoint presentations, you name it. So that is essentially the data plan that  
I urge my students to do.. Very simple, use this ELN. And when you think you've got a decent  
result, click on the Publish button. If you're not sure you got a decent result, keep it just in case.  
If you're pretty sure you've got rubbish, delete it. And about half what they run gets deleted.  
About a half of what remains gets published in the repository. And the other remaining quarter,  
they keep it just in case three or four years time they need to show their PhD examiner something 
that the examiner has asked for some evidence. They did a calculation, but perhaps that didn't  
make it into their thesis or dissertation, et cetera. So in other words, our data management plan is 
very simple. Item number one, use ELN and item number two publish the relevant results to the  
data repository. Item number 3 site the DOI in all publications. And it doesn't have to be, in my  
sense, more detailed than that. And that probably doesn't need updating on a regular basis and  
probably covers most of what needs doing. So I'm no great fan of data management plans  
provided you keep them as simple as I've just said it.

In another study that I was involved in had retinal data. So this  
was just pictures that were taken using an ophthalmolo-scope. And we had a big debate there  
because we wanted to release that as a public data set. But then we freaked out about, “oh my  
God, you know, your retina is like a fingerprint and the pattern of veins like the vasculature and  
your retina is unique to every person.” So we had a big freak out about that. And what if  
somebody tries to relate the retinal data to this person? But I mean, our, our fears or whatever  
substantiated and I don't think anyone ever tried to do that, but it was in the back of our heads  
when we were designing this and when we are trying to create anonymization procedures for that 
data. So it was something we thought about, but we weren't exactly sure, I think, how to go about 
doing that in the most secure a possible way. We just kinda hoped we’re doing it securely. But I  
would say that would sum up my experience.
